elements.sent-2411-001: The method of fragments was recently proposed, and its effectiveness has been empirically shown for three specialised pickup and delivery problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose an enhanced fragment algorithm that for the first time, effectively solves the Pickup and Delivery Problem with Time Windows.
elements.sent-2411-003: Additionally, we describe the approach in general terms to exemplify its theoretical applicability to vehicle routing problems without pickup and delivery requirements.
elements.sent-2411-004: We then apply it to the Truck-Based Drone Delivery Routing Problem Problem with Time Windows.
elements.sent-2411-005: The algorithm uses a fragment formulation rather than a route one.
elements.sent-2411-006: The definition of a fragment is problem specific, but generally, they can be thought of as enumerable segments of routes with a particular structure.
elements.sent-2411-007: A resource expanded network is constructed from the fragments and is iteratively updated via dynamic discretization discovery.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we introduce two new concepts called formulation leveraging and column enumeration for row elimination that are crucial for solving difficult problems.
elements.sent-2411-009: These use the strong linear relaxation of the route formulation to strengthen the fragment formulation.
elements.sent-2411-010: We test our algorithm on instances of the Pickup and Delivery Problem with Time Windows and the Truck-Based Drone Delivery Routing Problem with Time Windows.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our approach is competitive with, or outperforms the state-of-the-art algorithm for both.
elements.sent-2411-012: Vehicle routing problems (VRPs) aim to calculate a set of vehicle routes that service geographically distributed requests at minimum cost.
elements.sent-2411-013: They were first introduced by \cite{firstvrp to determine how to route fuel trucks from a depot to service demand at fuel stations.}
elements.sent-2411-014: One of the simplest VRPs is the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP).
elements.sent-2411-015: It can be modelled on a complete directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V = V'\cup\{0,n+1\}$ is the set of vertices and $E$ is the set of edges.
elements.sent-2411-016: Each vertex $i\in V'$ represents a request with weight $q_i$.
elements.sent-2411-017: Vertices $0$ and $n+1$ represent the depot at the start and the end of vehicle routes.
elements.sent-2411-019: An infinite fleet of homogeneous vehicles is assumed, each with capacity $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the CVRP, a route is an elementary path $\rr=(i_1=0,i_2,...,i_k=n+1)$ such that the vehicle capacity is not violated, $\sum_{j=1 ^k q_{i_j}\leq Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The cost of a route is the sum of its edge costs.
elements.sent-2411-022: The CVRP seeks to minimize the cost of the routes used to service each request $i\in V'$ exactly once.
elements.sent-2411-023: There are numerous extensions to this that model real world applications.
elements.sent-2411-024: For example, request service time windows can be included in which case the problem is called the Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (VRPTW).
elements.sent-2411-025: The Pickup and Delivery Problem with Time Windows (PDPTW) further extends the VRPTW by coupling requests into pickup and delivery pairs.Further qualifications are added to the definition of a route with each extension.
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, we define a route in the following generic way.
elements.sent-2411-027: A route is an elementary path $\rr = (i_1=0,i_2,...,i_k=n+1)$ in $G$ that satisfies the problem specific rules of the VRP in question.
elements.sent-2411-028: Further extensions such as multiple depots and finite heterogeneous vehicle fleets have also been considered, though these require a more complex definition of a route.
elements.sent-2411-029: The methods we proposed in this work assume the above definition for simplicity, though they can be extended to solve other VRPs.
elements.sent-2411-030: The reader is referred to \cite{vrptax1,vrptax2, vrptax3 and \cite{vrptax4 for extensive summaries of VRP variants.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: VRPs can be solved using arc-flow formulations where binary variables $x_e$ represent a vehicle traversing edge $e\in E$.
elements.sent-2411-032: These have a polynomial number of variables and constraints and a weak linear relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-033: This formulation consequently has an exponential number of variables, but usually a stronger linear relaxation than the arc-flow formulation.
elements.sent-2411-034: The set partitioning, or route formulation has a binary variable $\lambda_\rr$ for each route $\rr$ in the set of all possible routes $\R$.
elements.sent-2411-035: For each request $i\in V'$, we let $a^i_\rr$ be one if route $\rr$ services request $i$ and zero otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-036: Thus, the formulation, SPP, is the following: \begin{equation}\min \sum_{\rr\in\R} c_\rr\lambda_\rr \label{rbfo} s.t. \sum_{\rr\in\R}a^i_\rr\lambda_\rr = 1, \forall i\in V'\label{rbfc1} \lambda_\rr\in\{0,1\}, \forall \rr\in\R\label{rbfc2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: Objective function (\ref{rbfo ) minimizes the cost of the routes used.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Constraints (\ref{rbfc1 ) ensure each request is covered once.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The domain of the variables is defined by constraints (\ref{rbfc2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-040: Whilst many vehicle routing problems require extra constraints (for example, a limit on the number of vehicles), we omit them for the sake of clarity in the rest of Sections \ref{introduction and \ref{algorithm .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: As stated by \cite{bnpvrps , an advantage of formulating VRPs in this way is that many of the constraints are implicitly satisfied by each route and need not be included in the formulation.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This usually results in route formulations having better lower bounds than their arc-flow counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-043: The price paid is a huge number of variables which are often impossible to include all at once.
elements.sent-2411-044: Branch-and-price-and-cut (BPC) algorithms are designed to circumvent this.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this section we cover the main component of BPC algorithms, namely, column generation (CG).
elements.sent-2411-046: We refer the reader to \cite{bnp1 , \cite{cg , \cite{bnp2 and \cite{bnpvrps for in depth reviews of BPC algorithms.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: A BPC algorithm is a branch-and-bound (BB) algorithm where lower bounds are calculated via CG at nodes of the search tree.
elements.sent-2411-048: At each node, the linear relaxation of SPP superimposed with the node's set of branching constraints is referred to as the master problem (MP).
elements.sent-2411-049: We refer to the linear relaxation of SPP before any branching decisions are made as the root MP.
elements.sent-2411-050: CG solves MP by iteratively solving a restricted MP (RMP) and then a pricing subproblem.
elements.sent-2411-051: At the root node the RMP associated with SPP and the MP is given by \begin{equation}\min \sum_{\rr\in\R'} c_\rr\lambda_\rr \label{rmpo} s.t. \sum_{\rr\in\R'}a^i_\rr\lambda_\rr = 1, \forall i\in V'\label{rmpc1} \lambda_\rr\geq 0, \forall \rr\in\R'\label{rmpc2},\end{equation} where $\R'$ is a small subset of the routes in $\R$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Finding an optimal primal solution, $(\lambda_\rr)_{\rr\in\R'}$, yields an optimal dual solution $(\pi_i)_{i\in V'}$ to RMP's dual linear program (LP).
elements.sent-2411-053: The feasible MP solution that is obtained by setting $\lambda_\rr=0$ for all $\rr\in\R\backslash\R'$ is optimal if all routes $\rr\in\R\backslash\R'$ have non-negative reduced cost, $\bar{c _\rr = c_\rr - \sum_{i\in V' a^i_\rr\pi_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Hence, the pricing problem (PP) is solved to find negative reduced cost routes to add to $\R'$: \begin{equation}\min_{\rr\in\R\backslash\R'} c_\rr - \sum_{i\in V'}a^i_\rr\pi_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-055: If solving PP yields one or multiple negative reduced cost routes, they are added to $\R'$ and the process repeats.
elements.sent-2411-056: Otherwise, it terminates with an optimal MP solution.
elements.sent-2411-057: Subproblem PP is an elementary shortest path problem with resource constraints (ESPPRC) on $G$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The resources are quantities, such as time, load or distance, that vary as a route is traversed according to resource extension functions (REFs) which are defined for each resource and for each edge $(i,j)\in E$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Each resource has lower and upper bounds that must be satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-060: If the resource constraints permit cycles, one must carefully disallow them or relax the elementarity requirement, in which case the PP is solved as a shortest path problem with resource constraints (SPPRC).
elements.sent-2411-061: The SPPRC can be solved by a pseudo-polynomial algorithm whilst the ESPPRC is NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, \cite{ESPPRC1 show that using non-elementary routes may result in significantly lower quality bounds from MP.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Thus, significant focus has been placed on designing elementarity relaxations that balance the computational complexity of PP and the quality of MP's lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-065: Note that when SPPRC is used, the definition of $a^i_\rr$ is modified to be the number of times route $\rr\in\R$ visits request $i\in V'$.
elements.sent-2411-066: An SPPRC is solved by maintaining $\P$, a set of resource feasible paths in G that each start at $0$.

elements.sent-2411-319: Current state-of-the-art fragment methods handle this in two steps:.
elements.sent-2411-320: They include timed copies of nodes that are a fixed duration $\delta$ apart.

elements.sent-2411-323: Remaining underestimating chains are eliminated by feasibility cuts in a BC framework.
elements.sent-2411-325: Increasing the number of timed nodes clearly strengthens RFN's linear relaxation because it removes fractional underestimating chains from participating in the solution.
elements.sent-2411-326: Additionally, it reduces the number of underestimating chain eliminations needed for convergence.
elements.sent-2411-327: However, it also increases the number of flow conservation constraints (\ref{rfnc3 ) and timed fragment variables which may cause inefficiency by slowing down the processing of BB nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-328: We call this the relaxation-size trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-329: Efficient fragment methods therefore rely on finding a small enough fragment formulation that has a strong enough relaxation to be solved quickly.
elements.sent-2411-330: For the PDPTW, no such fragment formulation has been found until this work.
elements.sent-2411-331: Sections \ref{ddd -\ref{cere detail three enhancements that enable such a fragment formulation.}}
elements.sent-2411-332: The first contributes by reducing the number of resourced nodes needed for RFN to be sufficiently strong, by using DDD.
elements.sent-2411-333: This alone is not enough for some hard instances however, where sufficient strength is impossible no matter how many resourced nodes are included.
elements.sent-2411-334: One major contributing factor to the weakness is that non-elementary paths can be represented by fractional chains in the solution of RFN's linear relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-335: Clearly, basing the fragments on longer paths would reduce the impact of this, and may also remove underestimating chains, resulting in further strengthening of RFN.
elements.sent-2411-336: In fact, with sufficient increase in the path length of fragments, RFN's linear relaxation would produce a bound equal to that of the route formulation.
elements.sent-2411-337: See \cite{pstep2 's discussion on this phenomenon in their p-step formulation of the CVRP.}
elements.sent-2411-338: However, naively increasing fragment path length exponentially increases the number of resourced fragment variables and therefore would be likely to only exacerbate the relaxation-size trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-339: The second and third enhancements therefore aim to lengthen the paths of fragments without significantly increasing the number of variables.
elements.sent-2411-340: This is achieved through a combination of dynamically concatenating fragments, and using a well known BPC variable fixing technique to remove many of the resourced fragment variables.
elements.sent-2411-341: The main drawback of the static networks used by previous fragment methods is that they may include many unnecessary resourced nodes.
elements.sent-2411-342: On the other hand, some nodes might not have a sufficient number of resourced copies, leading to the elimination of too many underestimating chains.
elements.sent-2411-343: We handle these problems by implementing \cite{ddd 's DDD.}
elements.sent-2411-344: It works by repeatedly solving RFN and adding resourced nodes to eliminate underestimating chains in integer solutions.
elements.sent-2411-345: The construction of the network is therefore guided by successive solutions.
elements.sent-2411-346: Using DDD to eliminate underestimating chains requires multiple IPs to be solved.
elements.sent-2411-347: However, DDD has two advantages over BC on a static network.
elements.sent-2411-348: Firstly, $\N$ can start with few resourced nodes.
elements.sent-2411-349: Since resourced nodes are only added to remove underestimating chains as needed, DDD often converges to optimality before $|V|$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-350: This is not the case when a static network is used because a prohibitive number of resource nodes may be needed to ensure that few enough underestimating chains need eliminating.
elements.sent-2411-351: Furthermore, adding resourced nodes often removes more related underestimating chains than the lifted feasibility cuts in \cite{frag3 .}

elements.sent-2411-369: Our second proposed enhancement is to use a well known variable fixing technique of BPC algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-370: It alleviates the relaxation-size trade-off by reducing the size of the fragment formulation through filtering out many resourced fragments that cannot be in an optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-371: The technique relies on the following well known theorem that is used in many applications for solving IPs.
elements.sent-2411-372: Suppose we have an IP, $P:=\{\min c^\top x: Ex=b, x\in \mathbb{Z ^d_+\}$ and an upper bound, $z_{ub}$.}
elements.sent-2411-373: The LP $D:=\{\max y^\top b : y^\top E \leq c\}$ is the dual problem of the linear relaxation of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-374: Given a feasible solution to $D$, $y^*$, the reduced cost of variable $x_j$ is $\bar{c _j=c_j - y^{*\top E_j$ for each $j=1,...,d$ where $E_j$ is the column in $E$ corresponding to variable $x_j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-375: The objective value of $D$ at $y^*$ is a valid lower bound on $P$, $z_{lb = y^{*\top b$.}}
elements.sent-2411-376: Given variable $x_j$ for some $j\in\{1,...,d\}$, if $\bar{c _j > z_{ub - z_{lb}$ then $x_j = 0$ in all optimal solutions of $P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-377: This result is used directly on the fragment formulation by \cite{frag3 for the VRP they focus on.}
elements.sent-2411-378: However for the PDPTW and possibly other VRPs, the linear relaxation of RFN does not possess a strong enough lower bound and $z_{ub - z_{lb}$ is too large for $|\W|$ to be reduced sufficiently.}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Enhancement 3: Column Enumeration for Row Elimination
elements.sent-2411-422: The effective filtering of FL provides the foundation for a third enhancement that strengthens RFN by lengthening the fragment paths.
elements.sent-2411-423: As previously mentioned, doing so causes an exponential increase in the number of variables.
elements.sent-2411-424: However, many of the new variables can be filtered out using FL.
elements.sent-2411-425: Because of the particular way fragments are lengthened, the enhancement also reduces the number of flow conservation constraints (\ref{rfnc3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-426: Therefore, the final enhancement, column enumeration for row elimination (CERE), alleviates the relaxation-size trade-off by strengthening RFN and reducing the number of constraints, without risking the number of variables becoming too large.
elements.sent-2411-427: The idea is to concatenate fragment paths end-to-end.
elements.sent-2411-428: Given a node $\eta\in \HH$, CERE enumerates all fragments that can be constructed by joining a fragment that ends at $\eta$ and one that starts at $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-429: Let $\F_\eta^+$ and $\F_\eta^-$ be the set of fragments that start and end at $\eta$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-430: Let $\F_\eta^\cup$ be the set of fragments obtained by joining each in $\F_{\eta ^-$ with each in $\F_{\eta ^+$.}}
elements.sent-2411-431: That is, each $\f\in\F_\eta^\cup$ has path $\p_\f = \p_{\f_1}\oplus \p_{\f_2}$ and $L^1_\f = L^1_{\f_1}$ for some $\f_1\in\F_\eta^-$ and $\f_2\in\F_\eta^+$.
elements.sent-2411-432: Clearly $\F_\eta^\cup$ can replace $\F_\eta^+$ and $\F_\eta^-$ and each route will still have a representation in the network.
elements.sent-2411-433: In the event that the replacement occurs, we say node $\eta$ is absorbed by CERE.
elements.sent-2411-434: This is because no fragment in $(\F\cup\F_\eta^\cup)\setminus(\F_\eta^-\cup\F_\eta^+)$ starts or ends at $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-435: Thus, no resourced copies of $\eta$ are needed and $|\V|$ is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-436: We only perform CERE on nodes $\eta$ that do not cause a significant increase in the number of fragments.
elements.sent-2411-437: For each node $\eta\in\HH$ we enumerate set $\F_\eta^\cup$ and filter out members using FL.
elements.sent-2411-438: The node is absorbed if the number of fragments increases less than a chosen integer $\mathtt{MaxIncrease}$.
elements.sent-2411-439: This parameter controls the aggressiveness of CERE.
elements.sent-2411-440: Larger values result in more fragments with longer paths and setting it sufficiently large will result in route enumeration and the fragment formulation simplifies to its route counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-441: Choosing moderate values strengthens RFN and reduces the number of constraints significantly, without the number of variables becoming too large.
elements.sent-2411-442: While the three enhancements are intended for improving the fragment approach on the PDPTW, they lead to a general framework that may theoretically work on VRPs other than PDPs.
elements.sent-2411-443: For instance, the combination of FL and CERE fixes the problem of fragment paths being too limited in length for RFN to be sufficiently strong.
elements.sent-2411-444: Constructing the network with DDD enables the fragment approach to handle problems with wider resource windows at each request, since resourced nodes are added sparingly.
elements.sent-2411-445: In essence, the approach offers a solution method that is an alternative to BPC, which is theoretically applicable to many VRPs.
elements.sent-2411-446: Whilst BPC seeks to solve a strong formulation even though a bespoke BB framework is invoked and each BB node is computationally expensive to process, the enhanced fragment approach seeks to solve a strong formulation that is additionally constrained to be small enough for a commercial BB solver to handle.
elements.sent-2411-447: Each of the enhancements contributes to the automatic construction of such a strong formulation.
elements.sent-2411-448: The advantage of using a commercial BB solver is that more effective branching and cutting plane strategies can be used and significantly larger BB trees can be explored.
elements.sent-2411-449: Further research is needed to determine whether other VRPs can be more efficiently solved by this new strategy.
